title: Unified Tooling — Copilot and the Thing

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you will explore how GitHub Copilot acts as your central assistant for navigating repositories, documentation, and project information, all without writing a single line of code.

The guide panel will update automatically as you advance through the slides. The glowing edge and arrow will show you where to look.

steps.title: Open the Explorer Sidebar
steps.instruction:
We have opened the Explorer sidebar for you. This panel shows every file and folder in the current project.

Take a moment to browse the folder structure. Notice folders, configuration files, and files like README.md. In a unified platform, all of these live alongside the code in one place.

steps.title: Open the README
steps.instruction:
In the Explorer, find and click on the README.md file to open it in the editor.

README files are the front door to any project. They describe what the project does, how to set it up, and who maintains it. This is one of the most valuable files for non-developers to read.

steps.title: Close the Sidebar
steps.instruction:
You have explored the project structure and found the README. The Explorer sidebar is your starting point whenever you need to browse a project.

You can reopen it any time with Ctrl+Shift+E.

steps.title: Open Copilot Chat
steps.instruction:
We have opened Copilot Chat for you. You will see the chat panel on the right side of your screen.

This is where you will ask Copilot questions in plain English. No special syntax or commands are needed; just type what you want to know.

steps.title: Ask Your First Question
steps.instruction:
In the Chat input box, type:

What is GitHub Copilot and what can it do for someone who is not a developer?

Press Enter and read the response. Notice that Copilot gives you a plain-language answer. No jargon required.

steps.title: Ask a Follow-Up
steps.instruction:
Now ask a follow-up question in the same conversation:

How is Copilot different from a regular web search?

Copilot remembers your previous message, so it can give a more focused answer. Conversations flow naturally, just like chatting with a colleague.
steps.tip: You do not need to start a new conversation for each question. Follow-up questions often produce better, more specific answers.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Ask About GitHub.com Features
steps.instruction:
In Copilot Chat, type:

What are GitHub Issues and how would a project manager use them?

Copilot explains the feature in plain language. This is the same kind of question you can ask on GitHub.com using the chat icon in the navigation bar.

steps.title: Ask for a How-To
steps.instruction:
Try a practical question:

How do I create a new issue in a GitHub repository?

Read the step-by-step instructions Copilot provides. Notice how it walks you through the process clearly, the same way a teammate would.

steps.title: Scope to Your Project
steps.instruction:
Now try a question scoped to your current project:

@workspace What files are in this project and what do they do?

The @workspace prefix tells Copilot to search across the entire project, not just the open file. This is how you discover what a repository contains without opening every file yourself.
steps.tip: On GitHub.com, you can scope your questions to a specific repository for more focused answers. In VS Code, @workspace achieves the same thing.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Ask for a Summary
steps.instruction:
Summarizing long content is one of Copilot's most useful skills. In the Chat panel, type:

Summarize the README file in this project in three bullet points

Copilot reads the file and distills it into key points. On GitHub.com, the same capability works for pull request threads and issue conversations with dozens of comments.

steps.title: Translate Technical Content
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to make technical content accessible:

Explain the project structure in this repository as if I am a non-technical stakeholder

This is exactly the kind of question that saves you from scheduling a meeting with a developer. Copilot bridges the gap between technical details and plain-language understanding.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Explore Without Editing
steps.instruction:
Ask Copilot to help you understand the project:

@workspace What does this project do? Give me a high-level overview.

Notice that Copilot reads the code, configuration files, and documentation to build an answer. You did not need to open or read any files yourself.

steps.title: Find Specific Information
steps.instruction:
Try a more targeted question:

@workspace Where is the main configuration for this project?

Copilot points you to the relevant file and explains what it does. This is how you navigate unfamiliar projects without asking a developer to walk you through it.

steps.title: Ask About Technologies
steps.instruction:
Type:

@workspace What technologies or tools does this project use?

Copilot identifies the programming languages, frameworks, and external services the project relies on. This is valuable context for project planning, onboarding, and stakeholder conversations.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Draft a Document
steps.instruction:
We have opened a new file for you. Let's use Copilot to write something. In the Chat panel, type:

Help me write a short project summary for a stakeholder update email. The project is about consolidating team tools into GitHub.

Read the response. Notice how Copilot produces polished, professional text you can copy and use immediately.

steps.title: Copy and Paste the Response
steps.instruction:
Hover over Copilot's response in the Chat panel. You will see a small Copy icon. Click it to copy the text, then paste it into the open file with Ctrl+V.

This is the basic workflow for using Copilot to write: ask, review, copy, and paste.

steps.title: Refine the Text
steps.instruction:
The first draft is rarely perfect. Ask Copilot to improve it:

Make that summary shorter and more conversational

Copilot refines the text based on your feedback. You can go back and forth as many times as you like until the tone and length are right.

steps.title: Close the File
steps.instruction:
You do not need to save this file. We have closed it for you.

You just used Copilot to draft, copy, and refine professional text, all without writing code. This same workflow works for release notes, meeting agendas, documentation, and any other text you need to produce.

steps.title: Ask a Status Question
steps.instruction:
Imagine you need to report on a project. In Copilot Chat, type:

@workspace What is the current state of this project? List any open tasks or areas that still need work.

Copilot scans the project files and documentation to build a picture. On GitHub.com, this same question could draw from Issues, pull requests, and milestones across your entire organization.

steps.title: Format for Stakeholders
steps.instruction:
Take the answer and ask Copilot to reformat it:

Turn that into a three-sentence status update I can share in a Teams message

This two-step pattern (ask for raw information, then reformat) is one of the most practical workflows for non-developers. You gather data and shape it for your audience in seconds.
steps.tip: You can ask Copilot to format output as bullet points, a table, an email, or any other structure. Just describe what you need.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Simulate Onboarding
steps.instruction:
Pretend you just joined this team. In Copilot Chat, type:

@workspace I am new to this project. What do I need to know to get started?

Read the response carefully. Copilot gives you a starting guide based on the project's actual content. This is the experience a new hire would have if your documentation lives in GitHub.

steps.title: Dig Deeper
steps.instruction:
Try a follow-up question a new team member might ask:

@workspace Who are the main contributors or owners of this project?

If the repo includes author information, contribution history, or a CODEOWNERS file, Copilot surfaces it. The better your repository documentation, the better the onboarding experience.

steps.title: Audit Your Documentation
steps.instruction:
Ask one more question:

@workspace Is there anything missing from the documentation that a new team member would need?

This is a powerful way to audit your own repos. If Copilot cannot answer a basic onboarding question, that is a signal your documentation has a gap worth filling.
steps.tip: The quality of Copilot's answers reflects the quality of your documentation. Investing in clear README files and well-organized repos pays dividends.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Plan Your First Steps
steps.instruction:
Let's use Copilot to create a simple action plan. In the Chat panel, type:

Help me write a 5-step plan for moving my team's issue tracking from Jira to GitHub Issues

Read the plan Copilot generates. Notice how it gives you practical, actionable steps you can share with your team.

steps.title: Customize the Plan
steps.instruction:
Refine the plan for your situation:

We have about 200 open tickets and three team members. Adjust the plan for that scale.

Copilot tailors its advice based on the details you provide. The more context you give, the more specific and useful the answer becomes.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! 🎉 You have explored the VS Code interface, opened Copilot Chat, asked natural-language questions about GitHub features, used @workspace to explore a project, drafted and refined professional text, practiced status reporting and onboarding workflows, and planned your first consolidation steps.

You are ready for the next course in this track: Made Better Together.
steps.tip: Head back to the slides on the left for a recap of key takeaways, or close this panel to continue exploring on your own.
